nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or stagnant blackwater streams, flooded forest pools, oxbow lakes, shallow seasonally flooded lowland habitats with dense vegetation and leaf litter; tolerates hypoxic conditions via aerial respiration

decorPreferences: large smooth rocks and driftwood for cover
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce light intensity
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves) for blackwater aesthetics
minimal or no fine live planting (will be uprooted)

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore in captivity; overwhelmingly accepts only live fish prey. Some long-term captive specimens have been transitioned to large dead/fresh fish pieces or large meaty frozen foods (whole prawns, large earthworms, silversides) with considerable patience, but live feeders remain the primary staple for most individuals. Never accepts prepared dry foods. Feeding frequency: 2–3 times per week; avoid overfeeding to prevent fatty liver disease.

avoidWith: all other fish (will be eaten or fatally attacked)
any tankmate smaller than the wolf fish itself

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Erythrinus erythrinus. Verify URL resolves before selecting.

summary: A compact but ferociously predatory Amazonian trahira reaching ~35 cm. An obligate air-breather (labyrinthine suprabranchial organ) tolerating hypoxic water. Single-fish species-only setup mandatory; live-feeder diet rarely negotiated. Related to Hoplias but far smaller than the Black Wolf Fish (H. macrophthalmus, 1 m+).
careNotes: Erythrinus erythrinus is a member of the family Erythrinidae — the trahiras — a basal characiform lineage sharing ancestry with Hoplias and Hoplerythrinus. It should not be confused with the much larger Black Wolf Fish (Hoplias macrophthalmus, reaching over 1 m) that is suitable only for public aquaria; the Red Wolf Fish at ~35 cm is a more manageable but still demanding specialist fish. A minimum 300 L tank with a footprint of at least 150 cm is recommended for a single adult; larger is always better. The species possesses a modified suprabranchial chamber that functions as an accessory air-breathing organ, allowing survival in poorly oxygenated blackwater habitats; in captivity the fish must have unobstructed surface access to gulp air — a tightly fitting lid is also essential as it is a powerful jumper. Water chemistry should lean toward the soft, slightly acidic Amazon blackwater profile (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 2–10), though captive-bred individuals show some tolerance of neutral, moderately hard water. Maintain excellent filtration while avoiding strong turbulent flow; the species is accustomed to still or sluggish water. Keep only as a solitary specimen in a species-only aquarium — cohabitation with any other fish, regardless of size, leads to predation or severe aggression. The primary husbandry challenge is diet: most individuals will accept only live fish prey indefinitely, and ethical feeder-fish supply represents a significant long-term commitment. A minority of captive specimens can be weaned onto large whole dead prey items (fresh/frozen silversides, large prawns, earthworms) through patient target feeding, but this cannot be guaranteed and should not be assumed when acquiring the fish. Avoid copper-based medications. Partial water changes of 25–30% weekly are recommended.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria. Wild spawning occurs during the rainy season flood pulse in shallow, heavily vegetated flooded forest. Males are reported to guard egg clutches deposited among vegetation or leaf litter. Captive breeding requires a large, species-dedicated tank with a simulated dry/wet seasonal cycle (temperature drop and water level reduction followed by rainfall simulation), and compatible pair selection is hazardous given extreme inter-individual aggression. No reliable captive breeding protocol is documented in the hobby literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Erythrinus erythrinus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, water parameters (temperature 23–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 36–268 ppm / ~2–15 dGH), native range across tropical South America, habitat (slow/stagnant blackwater, flooded forest), diet (obligate piscivore, live prey), air-breathing organ notes, and tank requirements. Species-only solitary keeping confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Erythrinus erythrinus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Erythrinidae, Characiformes), adult size (max ~35 cm TL), native range (Amazon basin and surrounding drainages across South America), diet classification (carnivore/piscivore), and air-breathing classification. Confirmed family relationship to Hoplias and Hoplerythrinus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Wolf Fish Care
additional.notes: Trade/hobby perspective on wolf fish keeping; confirmed live-feeder dependency, solitary species-only requirements, large tank minimum, and aggressive piscivore temperament. Notes distinction between Erythrinus spp. and the much larger Hoplias macrophthalmus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Wolf Fish profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist advisory context for trahira predators; specialist-keeper designation, live-feeder diet challenge, air-breathing requirement (surface access), and escape risk (tight lid required) confirmed.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Erythrinidae overview
additional.notes: Family-level overview covering Erythrinus, Hoplerythrinus, and Hoplias genera; confirmed basal characiform phylogeny, air-breathing adaptation, piscivorous ecology, Amazon basin distribution, and captive husbandry challenges. Highlights that E. erythrinus is the smallest and most commonly encountered Erythrinidae in the trade.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Requires seasonal wet/dry cycle simulation and a compatible pair — extremely difficult given intense conspecific aggression. No established home-aquarium protocol exists.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Live-feeder dependent piscivore reaching 35cm. Single-fish species-only setup. Specialist large-fish commitment.
